2: Global energy demand has been rising rapidly. Currently, more than 84% of the world total energy demand is supported by non- renewable fossil resources, such as coal, oil, and natural gas (US EIA, 2013). These resources are not only limited in supply, but also have adverse effects on the environment due to the emission of greenhouse gases (GHGs) into the atmosphere. China is the world's second largest energy user and is facing the two severe challenges of energy shortages and an increased need for environmental pro- tection (Chang et al., 2003). One of the most promising alternatives

considered is to increase biofuel production such as biogas produc- tion through anaerobic digestion (AD) (Xu et al., 2013). Agricultural on-farm waste is a potential resource of AD, and a wide variety of on-farm waste is underutilized causing a serious waste of resources and other environmental problems. Most large-scale AD processes are operated at total solid (TS) contents of less than 15% and are classified as liquid-AD (L-AD) (Ge et al., 2016). In con- trast, solid-state anaerobic digestion (SS-AD) is suitable for cellu- losic biomass feedstock with low moisture content, such as agricultural residues (Li et al., 2011). Compared to L-AD, SS-AD has higher volumetric methane productivity, less wastewater gen- eration and can reduce energy requirements for heating (Xu et al., 2014). Thus, SS-AD has recently gained attention to process agri- cultural organic waste for energy production (Li et al., 2011).

<list>** Corresponding author at: Department of Food, Agricultural and Biological Engineering, The Ohio State University, 110 FABE Building, 1680 Madison Ave, Wooster, OH 44691, USA (A. Shah). 
* Corresponding author at: College of Resource and Environmental Science, China Agricultural University, Beijing 100193, PR China (G. Li).</list>
Digestate is the by-product of methane and heat production in a biogas plant, coming from organic wastes. Digestate is a fully fermented nutrient-rich material, especially containing a high

proportion of ammonium, which is available for plants. Moreover, it contains other macro- and micro-elements necessary for plant growth. Traditionally, digestate can be used as a fertilizer due to its nutrient content (Chen et al., 2014). However, biogas plants might lead to an oversupply of digestate in certain regions. If the agricultural area nearby is too small for adequate use of the diges- tate, surplus material has to be transported to regions with nutri- ent deficits (Rehl and Müller, 2011). The transportation of digestate, however, causes logistical problems since the trans- ported material has high water content leading to secondary envi- ronmental pollution (KTBL, 2005). For this reason, specific strategies have been implemented to manage digestate. Compost- ing is an effective and widely used method for organic residue treatment in which organic residues are transformed into low water content mature products for soil amendments and fertilizer. Integrated SS-AD and composting for agricultural waste treatment can achieve bioenergy production and matured fertilizer, but envi- ronmental impacts are not widely reported.

Geographic locations, characteristics of waste, energy sources, availability of disposal options, and market size for products derived from waste management differ widely, making it difficult to find optimal compromises in a technology for waste manage- ment (White et al., 1997; Mendes et al., 2004). Any technology can have a positive influence in one aspect, while having a negative influence in another aspect. Life cycle assessment (LCA) is a power- ful methodological tool for quantifying, evaluating, comparing and estimating the environmental impacts attributable to the life cycle of a product. It is also used for studying the environmental and potential impacts of a complex system, product or service through- out its life cycle (Rebitzer et al., 2004).

AD is considered as one of the best alternatives for agricultural organic solid waste management (Xu et al., 2013). Agostini et al. (2015) analyzed the environmental impacts of biogas systems run- ning on feedstock such as dairy manure, sorghum and maize. They found that using dairy manure as feedstock in AD had the highest GHG savings due to avoided emissions from the traditional storage and management of raw manure as organic fertilizer, while crops (corn and sorghum) can supplement nutrition (i.e. carbon) and facilitate the deployment of manure digestion. Wen et al. (2018) investigated the overall environmental effect of four treatment technologies (landfill, incineration, composting and AD) combined with the current main disposal technologies for municipal solid waste (MSW). They suggested that application of AD should be encouraged for its high removal efficiency of nitrogen, which had benefit to ecosystem quality improvement (ie. photochemical ozone and eutrophication). Biogas was recovered from AD and cogenerated to produce heat and electricity to replace electricity from the national grid. This substitution represented avoided nitro- gen emissions. And use of digestated instead of triple superphos- phate fertilizer also results in avoided P2O5 emissions linked to the production of mineral fertilizer (Gourdet et al., 2017). Battini et al. (2014) concluded that on-farm manure AD can effectively reduce GHG emissions and non-renewable energy consumption. According to Bacenetti et al. (2013) electricity production from bio- gas has huge potential for both energy savings and GHG emissions reductions. Further reductions of GHG emissions can be achieved by using an animal waste as feedstock. The result showed manure as feedstock can partially substitute energy crops and entails high credits for the avoided GHG emissions from undigested manure. Based on different studies discussed so far, anaerobic digestion with biogas production is a good treatment option in terms of atmospheric pollution emissions, photochemical ozone, acidifica- tion and eutrophication, particularly GHG savings.
Extensive research has been conducted over the past few dec- ades showing the benefits of implementing composting for organic solid waste treatment. Similar to the other types of agricultural

organic solid waste management, environmental benefits of com- posting is mainly from compost application (Martínez-Blanco et al., 2013). Martínez-Blanco et al. (2013) reported that farmland with long-term compost application show benefits of nutrient sup- ply, carbon sequestration, soil biodiversity, and soil workability. Boldrin et al. (2009, Boldrin et al. (2010)) conducted life cycle stud- ies analyzing global warming impacts of composting and environ- mental benefits of compost utilization. They found the overall global warming potential (GWP) for composting varies between significant savings (-9001 CO2-equivalents wet weight (ww)) to a net environmental impacts (300 kg CO2-equivalents ww). Significant factors are off-gas cleaning at the composting plant (in the enclosed systems) and the use of the compost (Boldrin et al., 2009). The main loads and main savings come from the use of the compost as a substitute for peat in the production of growth media. Avoiding the use of peat saves GHG emissions occurring during the extraction, transportation and use (i.e. degradation) of peat materials. Kong et al. (2012), using California-specific infor- mation, analyzed the GHG impacts of organics diversion through LCA. Their analysis resulted in net GHG reductions of 0.048, 0.065 and 0.073 ton CO2-equivalents per ton organic waste for windrow composting, aerated static pile composting, and anaero- bic digestion, respectively.

LCA has been used to investigate different agricultural waste processing technologies, such as anaerobic digestion, incineration, fermentation, and composting (Prapaspongsa et al., 2010; Jury et al., 2010; Lansche and Müller, 2012; Luo et al., 2014). However, most of the LCA studies only focus on manure or crop as feedstock material (Lijó et al., 2014a, 2014b; Zegada-Lizarazu and Monti, 2011; González-García et al., 2013), and limited research has eval- uated composting of the digestate from the AD system right after the completion of the AD process (Bustamante et al., 2013). It is difficult to draw conclusions from earlier studies on the compar- ison between different treatment technologies and scenarios because of different functional units and system boundaries, differ- ent assumptions and methodological choices, and often, the insuf- ficient detail provided on the used methodology (Evangelisti et al., 2014). The objective of this study is to investigate the environmen- tal impacts of the treatment of the agricultural wastes (tomato residues, dairy manure, and corn stover) comparing three different potential strategies: solid state anaerobic digestion (SS-AD), com- posting, and composting after SS-AD. For the system with SS-AD, energy will be recovered as heat and power from biogas produc- tion, and the solid residues/digestate will be used as fertilizer. For the scenario with composting, compost will be used as fertil- izer. For the scenario with composting after SS-AD, energy will be recovered as heat and power from biogas production and com- post is used as fertilizer.

However, most of the LCA studies only focus on manure or crop as feedstock material (Lijó et al., 2014a, 2014b; Zegada-Lizarazu and Monti, 2011; González-García et al., 2013), and limited research has eval- uated composting of the digestate from the AD system right after the completion of the AD process (Bustamante et al., 2013). It is difficult to draw conclusions from earlier studies on the compar- ison between different treatment technologies and scenarios because of different functional units and system boundaries, differ- ent assumptions and methodological choices, and often, the insuf- ficient detail provided on the used methodology (Evangelisti et al., 2014). The objective of this study is to investigate the environmen- tal impacts of the treatment of the agricultural wastes (tomato residues, dairy manure, and corn stover) comparing three different potential strategies: solid state anaerobic digestion (SS-AD), com- posting, and composting after SS-AD. For the system with SS-AD, energy will be recovered as heat and power from biogas produc- tion, and the solid residues/digestate will be used as fertilizer. For the scenario with composting, compost will be used as fertil- izer. For the scenario with composting after SS-AD, energy will be recovered as heat and power from biogas production and com- post is used as fertilizer.
5: Global energy demand has been rising rapidly. Currently, more than 84% of the world total energy demand is supported by non- renewable fossil resources, such as coal, oil, and natural gas (US EIA, 2013). These resources are not only limited in supply, but also have adverse effects on the environment due to the emission of greenhouse gases (GHGs) into the atmosphere. China is the world's second largest energy user and is facing the two severe challenges of energy shortages and an increased need for environmental pro- tection (Chang et al., 2003). One of the most promising alternatives

considered is to increase biofuel production such as biogas produc- tion through anaerobic digestion (AD) (Xu et al., 2013). Agricultural on-farm waste is a potential resource of AD, and a wide variety of on-farm waste is underutilized causing a serious waste of resources and other environmental problems. Most large-scale AD processes are operated at total solid (TS) contents of less than 15% and are classified as liquid-AD (L-AD) (Ge et al., 2016). In con- trast, solid-state anaerobic digestion (SS-AD) is suitable for cellu- losic biomass feedstock with low moisture content, such as agricultural residues (Li et al., 2011). Compared to L-AD, SS-AD has higher volumetric methane productivity, less wastewater gen- eration and can reduce energy requirements for heating (Xu et al., 2014). Thus, SS-AD has recently gained attention to process agri- cultural organic waste for energy production (Li et al., 2011).

* Corresponding author at: College of Resource and Environmental Science, China Agricultural University, Beijing 100193, PR China (G. Li).</list>
Digestate is the by-product of methane and heat production in a biogas plant, coming from organic wastes. Digestate is a fully fermented nutrient-rich material, especially containing a high

proportion of ammonium, which is available for plants. Moreover, it contains other macro- and micro-elements necessary for plant growth. Traditionally, digestate can be used as a fertilizer due to its nutrient content (Chen et al., 2014). However, biogas plants might lead to an oversupply of digestate in certain regions. If the agricultural area nearby is too small for adequate use of the diges- tate, surplus material has to be transported to regions with nutri- ent deficits (Rehl and Müller, 2011). The transportation of digestate, however, causes logistical problems since the trans- ported material has high water content leading to secondary envi- ronmental pollution (KTBL, 2005). For this reason, specific strategies have been implemented to manage digestate. Compost- ing is an effective and widely used method for organic residue treatment in which organic residues are transformed into low water content mature products for soil amendments and fertilizer. Integrated SS-AD and composting for agricultural waste treatment can achieve bioenergy production and matured fertilizer, but envi- ronmental impacts are not widely reported.

Geographic locations, characteristics of waste, energy sources, availability of disposal options, and market size for products derived from waste management differ widely, making it difficult to find optimal compromises in a technology for waste manage- ment (White et al., 1997; Mendes et al., 2004). Any technology can have a positive influence in one aspect, while having a negative influence in another aspect. Life cycle assessment (LCA) is a power- ful methodological tool for quantifying, evaluating, comparing and estimating the environmental impacts attributable to the life cycle of a product. It is also used for studying the environmental and potential impacts of a complex system, product or service through- out its life cycle (Rebitzer et al., 2004).

AD is considered as one of the best alternatives for agricultural organic solid waste management (Xu et al., 2013). Agostini et al. (2015) analyzed the environmental impacts of biogas systems run- ning on feedstock such as dairy manure, sorghum and maize. They found that using dairy manure as feedstock in AD had the highest GHG savings due to avoided emissions from the traditional storage and management of raw manure as organic fertilizer, while crops (corn and sorghum) can supplement nutrition (i.e. carbon) and facilitate the deployment of manure digestion. Wen et al. (2018) investigated the overall environmental effect of four treatment technologies (landfill, incineration, composting and AD) combined with the current main disposal technologies for municipal solid waste (MSW). They suggested that application of AD should be encouraged for its high removal efficiency of nitrogen, which had benefit to ecosystem quality improvement (ie. photochemical ozone and eutrophication). Biogas was recovered from AD and cogenerated to produce heat and electricity to replace electricity from the national grid. This substitution represented avoided nitro- gen emissions. And use of digestated instead of triple superphos- phate fertilizer also results in avoided P2O5 emissions linked to the production of mineral fertilizer (Gourdet et al., 2017). Battini et al. (2014) concluded that on-farm manure AD can effectively reduce GHG emissions and non-renewable energy consumption. According to Bacenetti et al. (2013) electricity production from bio- gas has huge potential for both energy savings and GHG emissions reductions. Further reductions of GHG emissions can be achieved by using an animal waste as feedstock. The result showed manure as feedstock can partially substitute energy crops and entails high credits for the avoided GHG emissions from undigested manure. Based on different studies discussed so far, anaerobic digestion with biogas production is a good treatment option in terms of atmospheric pollution emissions, photochemical ozone, acidifica- tion and eutrophication, particularly GHG savings.
Extensive research has been conducted over the past few dec- ades showing the benefits of implementing composting for organic solid waste treatment. Similar to the other types of agricultural

organic solid waste management, environmental benefits of com- posting is mainly from compost application (Martínez-Blanco et al., 2013). Martínez-Blanco et al. (2013) reported that farmland with long-term compost application show benefits of nutrient sup- ply, carbon sequestration, soil biodiversity, and soil workability. Boldrin et al. (2009, Boldrin et al. (2010)) conducted life cycle stud- ies analyzing global warming impacts of composting and environ- mental benefits of compost utilization. They found the overall global warming potential (GWP) for composting varies between significant savings (-9001 CO2-equivalents wet weight (ww)) to a net environmental impacts (300 kg CO2-equivalents ww). Significant factors are off-gas cleaning at the composting plant (in the enclosed systems) and the use of the compost (Boldrin et al., 2009). The main loads and main savings come from the use of the compost as a substitute for peat in the production of growth media. Avoiding the use of peat saves GHG emissions occurring during the extraction, transportation and use (i.e. degradation) of peat materials. Kong et al. (2012), using California-specific infor- mation, analyzed the GHG impacts of organics diversion through LCA. Their analysis resulted in net GHG reductions of 0.048, 0.065 and 0.073 ton CO2-equivalents per ton organic waste for windrow composting, aerated static pile composting, and anaero- bic digestion, respectively.

For the system with SS-AD, energy will be recovered as heat and power from biogas produc- tion, and the solid residues/digestate will be used as fertilizer. For the scenario with composting, compost will be used as fertil- izer. For the scenario with composting after SS-AD, energy will be recovered as heat and power from biogas production and com- post is used as fertilizer.

organic solid waste management, environmental benefits of com- posting is mainly from compost application (Martínez-Blanco et al., 2013). Martínez-Blanco et al. (2013) reported that farmland with long-term compost application show benefits of nutrient sup- ply, carbon sequestration, soil biodiversity, and soil workability. Boldrin et al. (2009, Boldrin et al. (2010)) conducted life cycle stud- ies analyzing global warming impacts of composting and environ- mental benefits of compost utilization. They found the overall global warming potential (GWP) for composting varies between significant savings (-9001 CO2-equivalents wet weight (ww)) to a net environmental impacts (300 kg CO2-equivalents ww). Significant factors are off-gas cleaning at the composting plant (in the enclosed systems) and the use of the compost (Boldrin et al., 2009). The main loads and main savings come from the use of the compost as a substitute for peat in the production of growth media. Avoiding the use of peat saves GHG emissions occurring during the extraction, transportation and use (i.e. degradation) of peat materials. Kong et al. (2012), using California-specific infor- mation, analyzed the GHG impacts of organics diversion through LCA. Their analysis resulted in net GHG reductions of 0.048, 0.065 and 0.073 ton CO2-equivalents per ton organic waste for windrow composting, aerated static pile composting, and anaero- bic digestion, respectively.

LCA has been used to investigate different agricultural waste processing technologies, such as anaerobic digestion, incineration, fermentation, and composting (Prapaspongsa et al., 2010; Jury et al., 2010; Lansche and Müller, 2012; Luo et al., 2014). However, most of the LCA studies only focus on manure or crop as feedstock material (Lijó et al., 2014a, 2014b; Zegada-Lizarazu and Monti, 2011; González-García et al., 2013), and limited research has eval- uated composting of the digestate from the AD system right after the completion of the AD process (Bustamante et al., 2013). It is difficult to draw conclusions from earlier studies on the compar- ison between different treatment technologies and scenarios because of different functional units and system boundaries, differ- ent assumptions and methodological choices, and often, the insuf- ficient detail provided on the used methodology (Evangelisti et al., 2014). The objective of this study is to investigate the environmen- tal impacts of the treatment of the agricultural wastes (tomato residues, dairy manure, and corn stover) comparing three different potential strategies: solid state anaerobic digestion (SS-AD), com- posting, and composting after SS-AD. For the system with SS-AD, energy will be recovered as heat and power from biogas produc- tion, and the solid residues/digestate will be used as fertilizer. For the scenario with composting, compost will be used as fertil- izer. For the scenario with composting after SS-AD, energy will be recovered as heat and power from biogas production and com- post is used as fertilizer.
9: Global energy demand has been rising rapidly. Currently, more than 84% of the world total energy demand is supported by non- renewable fossil resources, such as coal, oil, and natural gas (US EIA, 2013). These resources are not only limited in supply, but also have adverse effects on the environment due to the emission of greenhouse gases (GHGs) into the atmosphere. China is the world's second largest energy user and is facing the two severe challenges of energy shortages and an increased need for environmental pro- tection (Chang et al., 2003). One of the most promising alternatives

considered is to increase biofuel production such as biogas produc- tion through anaerobic digestion (AD) (Xu et al., 2013). Agricultural on-farm waste is a potential resource of AD, and a wide variety of on-farm waste is underutilized causing a serious waste of resources and other environmental problems. Most large-scale AD processes are operated at total solid (TS) contents of less than 15% and are classified as liquid-AD (L-AD) (Ge et al., 2016). In con- trast, solid-state anaerobic digestion (SS-AD) is suitable for cellu- losic biomass feedstock with low moisture content, such as agricultural residues (Li et al., 2011). Compared to L-AD, SS-AD has higher volumetric methane productivity, less wastewater gen- eration and can reduce energy requirements for heating (Xu et al., 2014). Thus, SS-AD has recently gained attention to process agri- cultural organic waste for energy production (Li et al., 2011).

Digestate is the by-product of methane and heat production in a biogas plant, coming from organic wastes. Digestate is a fully fermented nutrient-rich material, especially containing a high

proportion of ammonium, which is available for plants. Moreover, it contains other macro- and micro-elements necessary for plant growth. Traditionally, digestate can be used as a fertilizer due to its nutrient content (Chen et al., 2014). However, biogas plants might lead to an oversupply of digestate in certain regions. If the agricultural area nearby is too small for adequate use of the diges- tate, surplus material has to be transported to regions with nutri- ent deficits (Rehl and Müller, 2011). The transportation of digestate, however, causes logistical problems since the trans- ported material has high water content leading to secondary envi- ronmental pollution (KTBL, 2005). For this reason, specific strategies have been implemented to manage digestate. Compost- ing is an effective and widely used method for organic residue treatment in which organic residues are transformed into low water content mature products for soil amendments and fertilizer. Integrated SS-AD and composting for agricultural waste treatment can achieve bioenergy production and matured fertilizer, but envi- ronmental impacts are not widely reported.

Geographic locations, characteristics of waste, energy sources, availability of disposal options, and market size for products derived from waste management differ widely, making it difficult to find optimal compromises in a technology for waste manage- ment (White et al., 1997; Mendes et al., 2004). Any technology can have a positive influence in one aspect, while having a negative influence in another aspect. Life cycle assessment (LCA) is a power- ful methodological tool for quantifying, evaluating, comparing and estimating the environmental impacts attributable to the life cycle of a product. It is also used for studying the environmental and potential impacts of a complex system, product or service through- out its life cycle (Rebitzer et al., 2004).

AD is considered as one of the best alternatives for agricultural organic solid waste management (Xu et al., 2013). Agostini et al. (2015) analyzed the environmental impacts of biogas systems run- ning on feedstock such as dairy manure, sorghum and maize. They found that using dairy manure as feedstock in AD had the highest GHG savings due to avoided emissions from the traditional storage and management of raw manure as organic fertilizer, while crops (corn and sorghum) can supplement nutrition (i.e. carbon) and facilitate the deployment of manure digestion. Wen et al. (2018) investigated the overall environmental effect of four treatment technologies (landfill, incineration, composting and AD) combined with the current main disposal technologies for municipal solid waste (MSW). They suggested that application of AD should be encouraged for its high removal efficiency of nitrogen, which had benefit to ecosystem quality improvement (ie. photochemical ozone and eutrophication). Biogas was recovered from AD and cogenerated to produce heat and electricity to replace electricity from the national grid. This substitution represented avoided nitro- gen emissions. And use of digestated instead of triple superphos- phate fertilizer also results in avoided P2O5 emissions linked to the production of mineral fertilizer (Gourdet et al., 2017). Battini et al. (2014) concluded that on-farm manure AD can effectively reduce GHG emissions and non-renewable energy consumption. According to Bacenetti et al. (2013) electricity production from bio- gas has huge potential for both energy savings and GHG emissions reductions. Further reductions of GHG emissions can be achieved by using an animal waste as feedstock. The result showed manure as feedstock can partially substitute energy crops and entails high credits for the avoided GHG emissions from undigested manure. Based on different studies discussed so far, anaerobic digestion with biogas production is a good treatment option in terms of atmospheric pollution emissions, photochemical ozone, acidifica- tion and eutrophication, particularly GHG savings.
Extensive research has been conducted over the past few dec- ades showing the benefits of implementing composting for organic solid waste treatment. Similar to the other types of agricultural

organic solid waste management, environmental benefits of com- posting is mainly from compost application (Martínez-Blanco et al., 2013). Martínez-Blanco et al. (2013) reported that farmland with long-term compost application show benefits of nutrient sup- ply, carbon sequestration, soil biodiversity, and soil workability. Boldrin et al. (2009, Boldrin et al. (2010)) conducted life cycle stud- ies analyzing global warming impacts of composting and environ- mental benefits of compost utilization. They found the overall global warming potential (GWP) for composting varies between significant savings (-9001 CO2-equivalents wet weight (ww)) to a net environmental impacts (300 kg CO2-equivalents ww). Significant factors are off-gas cleaning at the composting plant (in the enclosed systems) and the use of the compost (Boldrin et al., 2009). The main loads and main savings come from the use of the compost as a substitute for peat in the production of growth media. Avoiding the use of peat saves GHG emissions occurring during the extraction, transportation and use (i.e. degradation) of peat materials. Kong et al. (2012), using California-specific infor- mation, analyzed the GHG impacts of organics diversion through LCA. 11: The LCA followed the ISO 14,040 and 14,044 standards ((ISO, 2006a, 2006b). LCA was conducted using the OpenLCA software (version 1.5.0), which is an open source LCA software developed by Green Delta, Germany. The data used for this study are based on Ecoinvent 3.2 database, relevant literature, and the experiments on AD, composting, and integrated SS-AD and composting of on- farm organic residues wastes (tomato residues, dairy manure, and corn stover).

Tool for the Reduction and Assessment of Chemical and other environmental Impacts (TRACI) 2.1 method was selected for impact assessment. The details of TRACI 2.1 is provided by EPA (United States Environmental Protection Agency) (2017). Five impact categories selected based on their relevance for on-farm waste management were: global warming potential (GWP)

(expressed in kg CO2-equivalants (eq.), and are based on the emis- sions of CO2, CH4, and N20), acidification potential (AP) (expressed in kg SO2-eq., including emission of NH3, NOx, and SO2), eutrophi- cation potential (EP) (expressed in kg N-eq., including emission of NH3, NOx, and leaching of NO), ecotoxicity potential (ETP) (expressed in CTUe (Comparative Toxic Units ecotoxicity)), and resource depletion (RD) (expressed in MJ surplus). Although TRACI 2.1 is more applicable to the U.S. situation, the impact categories of TRACI 2.1 are recognized as being of significance in China, espe- cially for fossil fuel and the effect to land and water.

(expressed in kg CO2-equivalants (eq.), and are based on the emis- sions of CO2, CH4, and N20), acidification potential (AP) (expressed in kg SO2-eq., including emission of NH3, NOx, and SO2), eutrophi- cation potential (EP) (expressed in kg N-eq., including emission of NH3, NOx, and leaching of NO), ecotoxicity potential (ETP) (expressed in CTUe (Comparative Toxic Units ecotoxicity)), and resource depletion (RD) (expressed in MJ surplus). 13: The goal of the LCA study was to quantify and compare different on-farm organic solid waste treatment strategies, including anaer- obic digestion, composting and integrated SS-AD and composting system. Five waste management systems based on three treatment methods (AD, composting, and AD + composting) for on-farm organic residues were selected.

In the AD system, feedstock and inoculum are fed into a diges- tion tank to produce biogas and digestate. The biogas is used for bioenergy production (electricity and heat). The digestate is sepa- rated into solid and liquid fractions. The liquid digestate goes through wastewater treatment, and the solid digestate can be used as an organic fertilizer to displace the mineral fertilizers. In the composting system, the feedstock and inoculum are treated in a composting plant to produce compost. The compost is used as an organic fertilizer for crops on the farm. In integrated SS-AD and composting system, feedstock and inoculum are fed into a diges- tion tank to produce biogas and digestate. The biogas is used for heat and electricity production. The digestate is treated in a com- posting plant with the addition of corn stover as a bulking agent.
14: In a multifunctional process analysis, different functional units (FU) can be used depending on the scope of the LCA. A functional unit is usually based on unit input or output (Rebitzer et al., 2004). Dairy manure is used in all the scenarios compared to other feedstocks (inputs). Also, there is not have a same output in all sce- narios. Therefore, the functional unit considered for this LCA study was the treatment of 1 ton (t) dairy manure (wet weight at mois- ture content of 82% as shown in Table 1) produced in the dairy farm.
15: The environmental footprints of all input processes of the entire life cycle from raw material transportation to compost/digestate

application in the farmland were included in this study. The sys- tems under assessment considered the processes (and correspond- ing inputs and outputs) involved: the transportation of corn stover and tomato residues up to the AD /composting plant, the pretreat- ment of corn stover and tomato residues, the feedstock mixing, the production, transportation and use of biogas, compost and diges- tate (Fig. 1). It was assumed that the composting plant and AD plant are located on-site dairy farms (4 km), which is common in China (Wang et al., 2012).

The system boundary also encompassed the impacts of the pro- duction, transportation, and application of avoided mineral fertil- izer as a result of using nutrients from compost/solid digestate as replacement. The inoculum considered in this study was effluent from a mesophilic L-AD system. L-AD effluent was used as the inoculum which is commonly used in small-scale biogas plants (Shi et al., 2014). The transportation, pump, and electricity used for inoculum was included in the system boundary. The avoided mineral fertilizers were assumed to be: urea (CO (NH2)2) for N, phosphorus pentoxide (P2O5) for P, and potassium oxide (K20) for K. The system boundary excluded the impacts of dairy cattle feeding as we assumed that a change in manure management would not affect dairy cattle feeding. Manure storage was not included in the system boundary. The excess heat from AD (i.e., heat produced in addition to the heat required to maintain the AD process) was not included as the possibilities for excess heat utilization are still limited (Dumont, 2010). All the electricity was assumed to come from China's national grid.
16: The environmental footprints of all input processes of the entire life cycle from raw material transportation to compost/digestate

application in the farmland were included in this study. All the electricity was assumed to come from China's national grid.
17: Five waste management systems were selected based on the three existing treatment methods for on-farm organic residues for this study (Fig. 1). The study compares the system with existing baseline system (liquid anaerobic digestion of dairy manure) with other alternatives for agricultural waste management. Further details on their different input and output attributes are provided in Tables 2 and 3, and have been briefly discussed in the following texts.

System 1 (S1): System 1 is based on the current most common practice for manure management in China, which includes liquid anaerobic digestion (L-AD) of dairy manure in a continuously stir- red tank reactor (CSTR) system (Deng et al., 2008). For this study, the TS of the mixture of feedstock, inoculum and water was consid- ered to be 8% with a feedstock to inoculum (F/I) ratio of 1 (volatile solids based). The process temperature is considered to be 35 °C. Since this is the most common practice for manure management in China, it has been considered as the baseline system for compar- ison of the other systems (i.e., systems 2 to 5).

Fig. 1. Overview of on-farm organic residues under 5 systems implementing 3 on-farm organic solid waste treatment strategies (anaerobic digestion, composting, and anaerobic digestion followed by composting.) (Note: System 1 (S1): Liquid AD (L-AD) of dairy manure; System (S2): SS-AD of dairy manure and corn stover; System 3 (S3): SS- AD of dairy manure, corn stover and tomato residues; System 4 (S4): Composting of dairy manure, corn stover and tomato residues; System 5 (S5): Integrated SS-AD and composting of dairy manure, corn stover and tomato residues). The lines with designations S1-S5 delineates different scenarios.

System 2 (S2): System 2 considers the co-digestion of corn stover with manure using SS-AD. Compared to conventional L- AD, SS-AD (TS > 15%) has many advantages such as higher volu- metric methane productivity and lower energy requirements (Li et al., 2011). However, the TS of dairy manure combined with inoculum is below 15% (Chen et al., 2010; Zhang et al., 2013). Corn stover with higher TS (Table 1) could be added to balance the TS of dairy manure. Furthermore, co-digestion of corn stover and dairy manure could balance the carbon-to-nitrogen ratio (C/N) of the feedstock (Li et al., 2009). Thus, solid state anaerobic co-digestion of dairy manure and corn stover was considered at TS of 15% at 35 °C using "garage-type" batch system in this study (Li et al., 2017). According to the previous studies, the mixing ratio of dairy manure and corn stover was 3:2 (volatile solids based) (Lehtomäki et al., 2007; Seppälä et al., 2013) with an F/I ratio of 4 (volatile solids based) (Li et al., 2017). In this system, the biogas is utilized by combined heat and power generation (CHP) engines to produce electricity and heat. The solid digestate is used as organic fertilizer, which can partially replace chemical fertilizers, and the remaining liquid fraction of digestate is treated in the wastewater facility.

System 3 (S3): This system considers the SS-AD of tomato resi- dues with dairy manure and corn stover. Co-digestion with multi- ple feedstock in AD plant have found to improve plant profitability and overcome a number of problems such as nutrient imbalance, rapid acidification and the presence of inhibiting compounds (Xu and Li (2012); Li et al., 2016). Several studies have shown that, the sensitivity and performance of the anaerobic digestion (AD) process may be improved by combining several feedstock streams. Most biogas systems in Germany co-digest between three and five feedstocks (Federal Agricultural Research Centre, 2005). Tomato residues have great potential as a co-substrate for biogas produc- tion due to its high organic fraction with about 75% of easily biodegradable matter (Bouallagui et al., 2005). Tomato residues are widely available in China since the rapid development of green-

house vegetable cultivation (Li et al., 2016). Solid state anaerobic co-digestion of tomato residues with dairy manure and corn stover was conducted at 15% TS at 35 °C using "garage-type" batch system in this study. The ternary mixture ratio is 24% corn stover, 36% dairy manure, and 40% tomato residues (volatile solids based) with an F/I ratio of 4 (VS based) (Li et al., 2017). Biogas and digestate are utilized in the same way as discussed in system 2.

System 4 (S4): In this system, dairy manure is delivered to a composting plant and then composted together with tomato resi- dues and corn stover. The initial moisture content is 65% and C/N ratio is 25, which is considered ideal for composting system (Guo et al., 2012; Kumar et al., 2010). The finished compost is used as organic fertilizer to replace part of the chemical fertilizer. This sys- tem is based on the actual operations of composting plant in Bei- jing China (Luo et al., 2014).

System 5 (S5): This system is based on system 3. After AD pro- cess, the digestate are treated in a composting plant with the addi- tion of corn stover as a bulking agent. Using corn stover as a bulking agent could balance the C/N ratio, moisture content and porosity of composts and thereby affects organic matter conver- sion, oxygen transfer and odor production and release (Zang et al., 2016). The initial moisture content of the mixture of diges- tate and corn stover was 65% which is commonly used in compost- ing (Guo et al., 2012). Biogas and digestate are utilized in the same way as discussed in system 2.

Thus, solid state anaerobic co-digestion of dairy manure and corn stover was considered at TS of 15% at 35 °C using "garage-type" batch system in this study (Li et al., 2017). According to the previous studies, the mixing ratio of dairy manure and corn stover was 3:2 (volatile solids based) (Lehtomäki et al., 2007; Seppälä et al., 2013) with an F/I ratio of 4 (volatile solids based) (Li et al., 2017). In this system, the biogas is utilized by combined heat and power generation (CHP) engines to produce electricity and heat. The solid digestate is used as organic fertilizer, which can partially replace chemical fertilizers, and the remaining liquid fraction of digestate is treated in the wastewater facility.

System 3 (S3): This system considers the SS-AD of tomato resi- dues with dairy manure and corn stover. Co-digestion with multi- ple feedstock in AD plant have found to improve plant profitability and overcome a number of problems such as nutrient imbalance, rapid acidification and the presence of inhibiting compounds (Xu and Li (2012); Li et al., 2016). Several studies have shown that, the sensitivity and performance of the anaerobic digestion (AD) process may be improved by combining several feedstock streams. Most biogas systems in Germany co-digest between three and five feedstocks (Federal Agricultural Research Centre, 2005). Tomato residues have great potential as a co-substrate for biogas produc- tion due to its high organic fraction with about 75% of easily biodegradable matter (Bouallagui et al., 2005). Tomato residues are widely available in China since the rapid development of green-

house vegetable cultivation (Li et al., 2016). Solid state anaerobic co-digestion of tomato residues with dairy manure and corn stover was conducted at 15% TS at 35 °C using "garage-type" batch system in this study. The ternary mixture ratio is 24% corn stover, 36% dairy manure, and 40% tomato residues (volatile solids based) with an F/I ratio of 4 (VS based) (Li et al., 2017). Biogas and digestate are utilized in the same way as discussed in system 2.

System 4 (S4): In this system, dairy manure is delivered to a composting plant and then composted together with tomato resi- dues and corn stover. The initial moisture content is 65% and C/N ratio is 25, which is considered ideal for composting system (Guo et al., 2012; Kumar et al., 2010). The finished compost is used as organic fertilizer to replace part of the chemical fertilizer. This sys- tem is based on the actual operations of composting plant in Bei- jing China (Luo et al., 2014).

System 5 (S5): This system is based on system 3. After AD pro- cess, the digestate are treated in a composting plant with the addi- tion of corn stover as a bulking agent. Using corn stover as a bulking agent could balance the C/N ratio, moisture content and porosity of composts and thereby affects organic matter conver- sion, oxygen transfer and odor production and release (Zang et al., 2016). The initial moisture content of the mixture of diges- tate and corn stover was 65% which is commonly used in compost- ing (Guo et al., 2012). Biogas and digestate are utilized in the same way as discussed in system 2.

System 2 (S2): System 2 considers the co-digestion of corn stover with manure using SS-AD. Furthermore, co-digestion of corn stover and dairy manure could balance the carbon-to-nitrogen ratio (C/N) of the feedstock (Li et al., 2009). Thus, solid state anaerobic co-digestion of dairy manure and corn stover was considered at TS of 15% at 35 °C using "garage-type" batch system in this study (Li et al., 2017). According to the previous studies, the mixing ratio of dairy manure and corn stover was 3:2 (volatile solids based) (Lehtomäki et al., 2007; Seppälä et al., 2013) with an F/I ratio of 4 (volatile solids based) (Li et al., 2017). In this system, the biogas is utilized by combined heat and power generation (CHP) engines to produce electricity and heat. The solid digestate is used as organic fertilizer, which can partially replace chemical fertilizers, and the remaining liquid fraction of digestate is treated in the wastewater facility.

System 3 (S3): This system considers the SS-AD of tomato resi- dues with dairy manure and corn stover. Co-digestion with multi- ple feedstock in AD plant have found to improve plant profitability and overcome a number of problems such as nutrient imbalance, rapid acidification and the presence of inhibiting compounds (Xu and Li (2012); Li et al., 2016). Several studies have shown that, the sensitivity and performance of the anaerobic digestion (AD) process may be improved by combining several feedstock streams. Most biogas systems in Germany co-digest between three and five feedstocks (Federal Agricultural Research Centre, 2005). Tomato residues have great potential as a co-substrate for biogas produc- tion due to its high organic fraction with about 75% of easily biodegradable matter (Bouallagui et al., 2005). Tomato residues are widely available in China since the rapid development of green-

house vegetable cultivation (Li et al., 2016). Solid state anaerobic co-digestion of tomato residues with dairy manure and corn stover was conducted at 15% TS at 35 °C using "garage-type" batch system in this study. The ternary mixture ratio is 24% corn stover, 36% dairy manure, and 40% tomato residues (volatile solids based) with an F/I ratio of 4 (VS based) (Li et al., 2017). Biogas and digestate are utilized in the same way as discussed in system 2.

System 4 (S4): In this system, dairy manure is delivered to a composting plant and then composted together with tomato resi- dues and corn stover. The initial moisture content is 65% and C/N ratio is 25, which is considered ideal for composting system (Guo et al., 2012; Kumar et al., 2010). The finished compost is used as organic fertilizer to replace part of the chemical fertilizer. This sys- tem is based on the actual operations of composting plant in Bei- jing China (Luo et al., 2014).

System 5 (S5): This system is based on system 3. After AD pro- cess, the digestate are treated in a composting plant with the addi- tion of corn stover as a bulking agent. Using corn stover as a bulking agent could balance the C/N ratio, moisture content and porosity of composts and thereby affects organic matter conver- sion, oxygen transfer and odor production and release (Zang et al., 2016). The initial moisture content of the mixture of diges- tate and corn stover was 65% which is commonly used in compost- ing (Guo et al., 2012). Biogas and digestate are utilized in the same way as discussed in system 2.

32: The composting and AD plants were assumed located near the dairy farm. The distance from dairy farm to AD/composting plant is 4 km, while from corn field and greenhouse to AD/ composting plant is 30 km. Feedstocks, digestate and compost are transported using trucks. Diesel consumption for transportation is affected by several factors, such as the type and speed of the trucks and the weight of the collected waste. In this study, the effect of the trans- portation is dependent on the weight of the collected waste and the average two way distance between the farmland and different facilities (Evangelisti et al., 2014).
33: The corn stover and tomato residues are chopped using a chop- ping machine during the pretreatment process. The energy requirements for the pretreatment and mixer operations can vary significantly depending on the machine used in the plant. After pretreatment process, the feedstock, inoculum, and water are fed into a mixing machine. In the model, the chopping machine with capacity of 10 m³/h and rated power of 7.5 kW, and the mixer with rated power of 4 kW were considered to be used for 1 h. The capac- ity of the pump used to transfer water and inoculum was 25 with 4 kW rated power. Data on the electricity required for pre- treatment and mixer are shown in Table 2.
34: In this process, the mixture (feedstock, water, and inoculum) are fed into the AD plant. The energy requirements for AD can vary significantly depending on the operating conditions of the plant, such as temperature, digester type and retention time. Mesophilic operations (operating at 35 °C) have been proven to be a reliable and acceptable option for digestion of on-farm waste because of its combined benefits of acceptable energy consumption, reliable process operation and favorable process performances (Zupancic and Ros, 2003). In China, most of the installed on-farm digesters for waste treatment are mesophilic (Deng et al., 2014). Thus, this LCA study assumed the use of a mesophilic digester.

SS-AD is characterized by comparatively high solid content of the feedstock to be digested, which is typically greater than 15% (Rapport et al., 2008; Li et al., 2011). AL-AD with the substrate con- taining 8% TS is considered in System 1, and a SS-AD with the sub- strate containing 15% TS is considered in Systems 2, 3, and 5, with TS content controlled by adding fresh water to the mixture. The operating time was considered to be 35 days per batch. Fugitive emissions of methane and other gases can occur throughout the AD plant, due to membrane cover permeability, leaky gaskets, maintenance, operations, and flaring or venting of biogas over- production (Liebetrau et al., 2010; Li et al., 2013a, 2013b). An aver- age value of 2% of the methane produced in the biogas plant is assumed to leak in most of the systems, and thus, is considered in this study (Berglund and Börjesson, 2006; Fruergaard and Astrup, 2011).

2.6.3.1. Anaerobic digestion plant. A batch, single-stage, mixed tank mesophilic reactor operation is assumed in the L-AD plant. The process has a continuous heat demand to maintain the digester temperature at 35 (Borello et al., 2013). A commonly used "garage-type" mesophilic reactor is assumed for the SS-AD plant (Sheets et al., 2015). The data on the electricity and heat require- ment considered in this work were mostly derived from (Li et al., 2017). The energy use during anaerobic digestion were estimated

by taking into account (1) heat required to raise the feedstock tem- perature to 35 °C, and maintain for 30 days (2) heat loss through walls, floor and cover of the digestion reactor, and (3) heat loss through the recycled water pipeline. Outputs from the digestion process were estimated based on the amount of biogas produced, the digestate, and the nutritional content in the digestate.

2.6.3.2. Use of biogas. The biogas produced is desulfurized and dehumidified and then converted to electrical and thermal energy by means of a combined heat and power (CHP) internal combus- tion engine. The CHP system is composed of multiple gas engines (serving as the power generation units) equipped with a heat recovery system, a boiler as the heat generation system, the electri- cal and thermal energy storage systems and a heat coil (Shang et al., 2017). For this analysis, the 200 kW generator from the Ecoinvent 3.2 was used. Like other study (Evangelisti et al., 2014; Zhao et al., 2009), the emission during desulfurization and dehu- midification was not considered, because H2S is present in small level (typical concentrations in the range of 0.2-0.6 vol%) and dehumidification is usually used for condensation of water or water removal, both of which have very low emissions (Schieder et al., 2003). The methane yields for the systems analyzed are based on (Li et al., 2017), which are 172.3 m³/t VS feed for system 1,291.6 VS feed for system 2, and 379.1 VS feed for systems 3 and 5.

The electrical and thermal conversion efficiencies of CHP inter- nal combustion engine considered in this study are 32% and 50%, respectively (Patterson et al., 2011). The electricity and heat gener- ated were calculated using Eqs. (1) and (2). E=n1x(Vxe)/3.6 (1)

is the total electricity being produced (kWh); N1 is the electri- cal conversion efficiency (32%); V is the methane used in CHP inter- nal combustion engine (m³); e is the calorific value of methane (35.9 MJ/m3). Heat produced by the CHP (2)

N2 is the thermal conversion efficiency (50%). The electricity produced is assumed to be fed to the national electricity grid. Thermal energy, in the cases analyzed here, is used solely to supply the necessary heat to the AD plant; the rest of the thermal energy is not used. This is the common practice for farm- based biogas plants (Agostini et al., 2015). Due to the various AD feedstock, CH4 constitute around 40-80% of the biogas ingredients. The average lower heating value of biogas is around 21.5 MJ/m³ at the standard temperature and pressure (around 34,300 kJ/m³) (Hosseini and Wahid, 2014). During combustion of 1 MJ equivalent of biogas (0.05 m³ of biogas with 63% CH4) in a CHP plant, 0.115 CO, 0.148 g NOx, 0.465 CH4 (for unburned CH4), and 0.105 g non- methane volatile organic compound (NMVOC) are emitted (Fruer- gaard et al., 2011; Evangelisti et al., 2014).

2.6.3.3. Treatment and use of digestate. Using the solid digestate as an organic fertilizer reduces the use of the mineral fertilizers. It is assumed that no nutrients (N, P, and K) are lost during the AD process (Evangelisti et al., 2014); therefore, the total nutrient con- tent in the digestate is the same as that in the feedstock in case of systems 1, 2, 3, and 5. The liquid digestate go through the waste water treatment, which was not included in the system boundary. Previous studies (Walsh et al., 2012) show minimal amount of N, P, and K pass through the liquid stream, thus the lost N, P, and K in liquid digestate was ignored in this study.
35: In this process, the mixture (feedstock, water, and inoculum) are fed into the AD plant. The energy requirements for AD can vary significantly depending on the operating conditions of the plant, such as temperature, digester type and retention time. Mesophilic operations (operating at 35 °C) have been proven to be a reliable and acceptable option for digestion of on-farm waste because of its combined benefits of acceptable energy consumption, reliable process operation and favorable process performances (Zupancic and Ros, 2003). In China, most of the installed on-farm digesters for waste treatment are mesophilic (Deng et al., 2014). Thus, this LCA study assumed the use of a mesophilic digester.

SS-AD is characterized by comparatively high solid content of the feedstock to be digested, which is typically greater than 15% (Rapport et al., 2008; Li et al., 2011). AL-AD with the substrate con- taining 8% TS is considered in System 1, and a SS-AD with the sub- strate containing 15% TS is considered in Systems 2, 3, and 5, with TS content controlled by adding fresh water to the mixture. The operating time was considered to be 35 days per batch. Fugitive emissions of methane and other gases can occur throughout the AD plant, due to membrane cover permeability, leaky gaskets, maintenance, operations, and flaring or venting of biogas over- production (Liebetrau et al., 2010; Li et al., 2013a, 2013b). An aver- age value of 2% of the methane produced in the biogas plant is assumed to leak in most of the systems, and thus, is considered in this study (Berglund and Börjesson, 2006; Fruergaard and Astrup, 2011).

2.6.3.1. Anaerobic digestion plant. A batch, single-stage, mixed tank mesophilic reactor operation is assumed in the L-AD plant. The process has a continuous heat demand to maintain the digester temperature at 35 (Borello et al., 2013). A commonly used "garage-type" mesophilic reactor is assumed for the SS-AD plant (Sheets et al., 2015). The data on the electricity and heat require- ment considered in this work were mostly derived from (Li et al., 2017). The energy use during anaerobic digestion were estimated

by taking into account (1) heat required to raise the feedstock tem- perature to 35 °C, and maintain for 30 days (2) heat loss through walls, floor and cover of the digestion reactor, and (3) heat loss through the recycled water pipeline. Outputs from the digestion process were estimated based on the amount of biogas produced, the digestate, and the nutritional content in the digestate.

2.6.3.2. Use of biogas. The biogas produced is desulfurized and dehumidified and then converted to electrical and thermal energy by means of a combined heat and power (CHP) internal combus- tion engine. The CHP system is composed of multiple gas engines (serving as the power generation units) equipped with a heat recovery system, a boiler as the heat generation system, the electri- cal and thermal energy storage systems and a heat coil (Shang et al., 2017). For this analysis, the 200 kW generator from the Ecoinvent 3.2 was used. Like other study (Evangelisti et al., 2014; Zhao et al., 2009), the emission during desulfurization and dehu- midification was not considered, because H2S is present in small level (typical concentrations in the range of 0.2-0.6 vol%) and dehumidification is usually used for condensation of water or water removal, both of which have very low emissions (Schieder et al., 2003). The methane yields for the systems analyzed are based on (Li et al., 2017), which are 172.3 m³/t VS feed for system 1,291.6 VS feed for system 2, and 379.1 VS feed for systems 3 and 5.

The electrical and thermal conversion efficiencies of CHP inter- nal combustion engine considered in this study are 32% and 50%, respectively (Patterson et al., 2011). The electricity and heat gener- ated were calculated using Eqs. (1) and (2). E=n1x(Vxe)/3.6 (1)

Fugitive emissions of methane and other gases can occur throughout the AD plant, due to membrane cover permeability, leaky gaskets, maintenance, operations, and flaring or venting of biogas over- production (Liebetrau et al., 2010; Li et al., 2013a, 2013b). An aver- age value of 2% of the methane produced in the biogas plant is assumed to leak in most of the systems, and thus, is considered in this study (Berglund and Börjesson, 2006; Fruergaard and Astrup, 2011).

2.6.3.1. Anaerobic digestion plant. A batch, single-stage, mixed tank mesophilic reactor operation is assumed in the L-AD plant. The process has a continuous heat demand to maintain the digester temperature at 35 (Borello et al., 2013). A commonly used "garage-type" mesophilic reactor is assumed for the SS-AD plant (Sheets et al., 2015). The data on the electricity and heat require- ment considered in this work were mostly derived from (Li et al., 2017). The energy use during anaerobic digestion were estimated

by taking into account (1) heat required to raise the feedstock tem- perature to 35 °C, and maintain for 30 days (2) heat loss through walls, floor and cover of the digestion reactor, and (3) heat loss through the recycled water pipeline. Outputs from the digestion process were estimated based on the amount of biogas produced, the digestate, and the nutritional content in the digestate.

2.6.3.2. Use of biogas. The biogas produced is desulfurized and dehumidified and then converted to electrical and thermal energy by means of a combined heat and power (CHP) internal combus- tion engine. The CHP system is composed of multiple gas engines (serving as the power generation units) equipped with a heat recovery system, a boiler as the heat generation system, the electri- cal and thermal energy storage systems and a heat coil (Shang et al., 2017). For this analysis, the 200 kW generator from the Ecoinvent 3.2 was used. Like other study (Evangelisti et al., 2014; Zhao et al., 2009), the emission during desulfurization and dehu- midification was not considered, because H2S is present in small level (typical concentrations in the range of 0.2-0.6 vol%) and dehumidification is usually used for condensation of water or water removal, both of which have very low emissions (Schieder et al., 2003). The methane yields for the systems analyzed are based on (Li et al., 2017), which are 172.3 m³/t VS feed for system 1,291.6 VS feed for system 2, and 379.1 VS feed for systems 3 and 5.

The electrical and thermal conversion efficiencies of CHP inter- nal combustion engine considered in this study are 32% and 50%, respectively (Patterson et al., 2011). The electricity and heat gener- ated were calculated using Eqs. (1) and (2). E=n1x(Vxe)/3.6 (1)

is the total electricity being produced (kWh); N1 is the electri- cal conversion efficiency (32%); V is the methane used in CHP inter- nal combustion engine (m³); e is the calorific value of methane (35.9 MJ/m3). Heat produced by the CHP (2)

N2 is the thermal conversion efficiency (50%). The electricity produced is assumed to be fed to the national electricity grid. Thermal energy, in the cases analyzed here, is used solely to supply the necessary heat to the AD plant; the rest of the thermal energy is not used. This is the common practice for farm- based biogas plants (Agostini et al., 2015). Due to the various AD feedstock, CH4 constitute around 40-80% of the biogas ingredients. The average lower heating value of biogas is around 21.5 MJ/m³ at the standard temperature and pressure (around 34,300 kJ/m³) (Hosseini and Wahid, 2014). During combustion of 1 MJ equivalent of biogas (0.05 m³ of biogas with 63% CH4) in a CHP plant, 0.115 CO, 0.148 g NOx, 0.465 CH4 (for unburned CH4), and 0.105 g non- methane volatile organic compound (NMVOC) are emitted (Fruer- gaard et al., 2011; Evangelisti et al., 2014).

2.6.3.3. Treatment and use of digestate. Using the solid digestate as an organic fertilizer reduces the use of the mineral fertilizers. It is assumed that no nutrients (N, P, and K) are lost during the AD process (Evangelisti et al., 2014); therefore, the total nutrient con- tent in the digestate is the same as that in the feedstock in case of systems 1, 2, 3, and 5. The liquid digestate go through the waste water treatment, which was not included in the system boundary. Previous studies (Walsh et al., 2012) show minimal amount of N, P, and K pass through the liquid stream, thus the lost N, P, and K in liquid digestate was ignored in this study.
41: A typical windrow composting facility is assumed in this study, designs of which were partly based on the previous studies (van

Haaren et al., 2010; Luo et al., 2014). Windrow composting is car- ried out in piles. The windrows are turned weekly to mix the feed- stock material, expose the material in the core to atmospheric air which can maintain high temperatures within the pile, and also allow some oxygen flow to the core. The energy demand of the composting plant was calculated based on data from Luo et al. (2014). Rotary mixer operated by diesel was assumed to be used for mixing the compost piles in our study. Methane (CH4), ammo- nia (NH3) and nitrous oxide (N20) have been considered as main emissions during the composting process (Jiang et al., 2011; Luo et al., 2012). Emissions data for the composting process used in systems 4 and 5 (dry matter content in initial compost was 35%) were obtained from measurements from 1.2 m³ composting trials, using the methodology developed by Jiang et al. (2011) and Luo et al. (2012). The amounts of compost product and the nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P), and potassium (K) were calculated on the basis of the results obtained from composting trials (dry matter content in the final compost was 65%) (Table 3).

43: Environmental impacts of transport processes have become a major issue of concern as transportation is the second major con- tributor to the overall greenhouse gas emissions in the United States and Europe (U.S. EPA, 2013; EC, 2011; Farkavcova et al., 2017). Hence, a reduction of environmental impacts of transport processes can help achieve the environmental targets. Thus, this scenario analysis was carried out. In system 5, 3 different feedstock are transported, and this system also combines AD and composting processes. Thus, scenario analysis was identified and performed based on system 5. It was assumed that the distance from origin of dairy manure to AD plant is 4 km, which is close to distance sug- gested by Pöschl et al. (2010). At European level, the EU Directive 2009/28/EC (Directive 2009/28/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council, 2009) on renewable energy sources mandates a progressive reduction of GHG emission in the production of bio- fuels and bio-liquids. Compared to fossil energy, it is recommended the maximum distance between the site of biomass production and that of energy conversion to be 70 km (Capponi et al., 2012). Since this analysis considers on-farm organic waste management techniques which would be of smaller capacity than that explained earlier, thus 60 km was considered in this study.
Different scenarios are summarized in Table 4, and are briefly discussed here.

Scenario 1 (Sc1): It was assumed the composting plant and AD plant located on dairy farms, the distance from dairy farms to AD plant is 4 km, and the distance from farmland and greenhouse to AD plant are both 60 km.
Scenario 2 (Sc2): The composting plant and AD plant located on farmland, the distance from farmland to AD plant is 4 km, and the distance from dairy farms and greenhouse to AD plant are both 60 km.
Scenario 3 (Sc3): The composting plant and AD plant located on greenhouse, the distance from greenhouse to AD plant is 4 km, and the distance from dairy farms and farmland to AD plant are both 60 km.

45: The global warming potential (GWP) of all the five systems had net negative values with net emissions reduction between -183 and -2900 kg CO2 eq. per functional unit (Fig. 2). In the presented results, positive values signify environmental footprints whereas negative values indicate environmental credits. The environmental credits of GWP indicated that the avoided emissions due to substi- tuting the use of fossil fuel with biogas converted to electrical and thermal energy, substituting chemical fertilizer with digestate, avoided incineration of corn stover that would otherwise be needed in the absence of biogas facility, and avoided landfill of tomato residues that would be needed in absence of biogas facility. Despite the fact that higher amount of corn stover and tomato resi- due applied entails higher environmental burdens associated to its transportation in S4 and S5, these two systems presented higher environmental credits due to avoided corn stover incineration. The reason for this is burning corn stover bears the greatest envi- ronmental burdens due to the higher emissions of carbon dioxide, methane, and nitrous oxide. It was evident that S5 is the most favorable alternative in terms of GWP, which is the most popular category indicator for estimating climate change impact (Guinée et al., 2002). Net GWP for S5 was negative and approximately 14.8-fold lower than that of the baseline system (S1). AD process itself had high contribution to GWP in different applicable systems (S1, S2, S3, and S5) ranging from 26 to 94%. This was mainly due to methane losses from the plant, and to construction and operation of AD plants. Composting process also contributed to GWP; and it is worth noting that there is a significant potential for GWP mit- igation, if AD is used prior to composting. Compared to S4, S5 had a 52% reduction in the GWP for the composting process.

47: Acidification potential (AP) is commonly related to the atmo- spheric pollution by S and N (e.g., NO or ammonia) because of human activities. S3 was the most favorable option in terms of the net AP (-3.1 SO2 eq. per functional unit), while the S4 had the highest net AP (21.5 eq .per functional unit) (Fig. 3). Com- posting was a dominant process contributing to the AP. The system with composting process (S4 and S5) had the net environmental burdens while S1 to S3 had net negative AP. The main reason for this was due to the emission of ammonia from the composting pro- cess. Similar finding was also reported by Agostini et al. (2015). They found that the emissions of ammonia are the largest contrib- utors accounting for the total impact, while system with AD pro- cess, ammonia emissions were practically counterbalanced by the emission credits.

The main processes contributing to the environmental credits of AP were avoided chemical fertilizer and avoided electricity, and avoided corn stover incineration. The contributions of avoided chemical fertilizer of all systems to AP are more than 31%. Though Chinese agriculture has intensified greatly since the early 1980s on a limited land area with large inputs of chemical fertilizers and other resources, fertilizer nitrogen (N) consumption reached 32.6

M nationally in 2007 (Guo et al., 2010). High levels of N fertiliza- tion can drive soil acidification both directly and indirectly. The avoided electricity contributed to 28-56% of AP in all five systems. Tzimas et al. (2007) have shown that the electricity mainly from fossil fuel power generation could cause a direct emissions of acid- ifying gases (i.e. a deeper reduction of SO2 and an increase of NOx) from fossil fuel -fired power plants. Also, the consumption of fuel resources, formation of wastes with adverse environmental impacts and by-products will increase. Corn stover used in each system can avoid corn stover burned in the open field. Sonesson et al. (2000) found that incineration has higher AP footprints com- pare to using the same feedstock in AD and composting. In this cat- egory, using corn stover in systems 4 and 5 to avoid corn stover incineration in the field shows advantage.

51: Eutrophication potential (EP) covers the potential impacts of elements above the environmental level (mainly N and P) that may result in adverse fluctuation of species composition and bio- mass production. According to the results based on EP, the solid state anaerobic digestion for organic matter clearly benefits the environment, especially in S3, which provides the highest net envi- ronmental impacts (Fig. 4). While the systems with composting process (S4 and S5) had net environmental burdens. This result showed that EP effect of the system was mainly caused by NH3 emissions during composting. There is a significant potential for

EP mitigation, if AD is used prior to composting (S5). Compared to S4, S5 yielded a 33% reduction in the NH3 emissions for the com- posting process, which led to the reduction in EP by 34% in S5 com- pared to S4. In scenarios 4 and 5 the corn stover is used for composting and AD process instead of being burnt in the field. The burnt corn stover also contain certains amount of N, P, and K which would minimize the chemical fertilizer requirement to some extent. The environmental burdens associated with removed nutri- ents as a result of used corn stover in scenarios 4 and 5 are shown in Fig. 4.

On the other hand, avoided chemical fertilizer has potentials for EP mitigation. In particular, direct emissions of chemicals related to chemical fertilizer showed a significant negative impact (Fig. 4). Especially in S4 and S5, more than 1.6 kg N eq per func- tional unit can be avoided when utilizing the nutrients from com- post/ solid digestate as a substitute for chemical fertilizer. The other high environmental credit was contributed by avoided elec- tricity in all scenarios with AD systems, which was mainly because using biogas produced electricity can replace electricity produced by fossil fuels. The EP of electricity produced by fossil fuels was 59.9 mg PO and 132.2 per KW (Pehnt, 2006; Evangelisti et al., 2014).
Except for S4, other systems had net negative ecotoxicity poten- tial (ETP). S3 had higher net ETP credits in comparison to the other
Eutrophication Potential (kg N eq/ of dairy manure)
Fig. 4. The eutrophication potential of 5 systems implementing 3 on-farm organic solid waste treatment strategies (anaerobic digestion, composting, and anaerobic digestion followed by composting.)

scenarios (Fig. 5). While S4 was against the preferred option and it was the only one system having environmental burdens, which is similar to the finding of the composting scenario always having environmental burdens in terms of ETP (Luo et al., 2012). Trans- portation, pretreatment, and removed nutrients were the main contributors to the ETP. Due to the additional materials and emis- sions associated with the construction and operation of vehicles, the transportation contributed to 25-63% of environmental bur- dens based on ETP in all five systems. Considering the electricity used in the chopping machine, contributions of pretreatment of both S4 and S5 to ETP are more than 13%. Facility construction, AD process, composting process, and wastewater treatment pro- cess also contributed to ETP. Avoided chemical fertilizer (compost and digestate can be used as organic fertilizer and partially replace chemical fertilizer) and avoided electricity largely contribute to environmental credits based on ETP. Especially avoided chemical fertilizer contributed to 55-95% of environmental credits based on ETP in all five systems. It is mainly because nickel (Ni) and Mer- cury (Hg) are more ecotoxic, which are commonly used in chemical fertilizer and electricity production (Abduli et al., 2011).

While S4 was against the preferred option and it was the only one system having environmental burdens, which is similar to the finding of the composting scenario always having environmental burdens in terms of ETP (Luo et al., 2012). Trans- portation, pretreatment, and removed nutrients were the main contributors to the ETP. Due to the additional materials and emis- sions associated with the construction and operation of vehicles, the transportation contributed to 25-63% of environmental bur- dens based on ETP in all five systems. Considering the electricity used in the chopping machine, contributions of pretreatment of both S4 and S5 to ETP are more than 13%. Facility construction, AD process, composting process, and wastewater treatment pro- cess also contributed to ETP. Avoided chemical fertilizer (compost and digestate can be used as organic fertilizer and partially replace chemical fertilizer) and avoided electricity largely contribute to environmental credits based on ETP. Especially avoided chemical fertilizer contributed to 55-95% of environmental credits based on ETP in all five systems. It is mainly because nickel (Ni) and Mer- cury (Hg) are more ecotoxic, which are commonly used in chemical fertilizer and electricity production (Abduli et al., 2011).
55: Eutrophication potential (EP) covers the potential impacts of elements above the environmental level (mainly N and P) that may result in adverse fluctuation of species composition and bio- mass production. According to the results based on EP, the solid state anaerobic digestion for organic matter clearly benefits the environment, especially in S3, which provides the highest net envi- ronmental impacts (Fig. 4). While the systems with composting process (S4 and S5) had net environmental burdens. This result showed that EP effect of the system was mainly caused by NH3 emissions during composting. There is a significant potential for

EP mitigation, if AD is used prior to composting (S5). Compared to S4, S5 yielded a 33% reduction in the NH3 emissions for the com- posting process, which led to the reduction in EP by 34% in S5 com- pared to S4. In scenarios 4 and 5 the corn stover is used for composting and AD process instead of being burnt in the field. The burnt corn stover also contain certains amount of N, P, and K which would minimize the chemical fertilizer requirement to some extent. The environmental burdens associated with removed nutri- ents as a result of used corn stover in scenarios 4 and 5 are shown in Fig. 4.

On the other hand, avoided chemical fertilizer has potentials for EP mitigation. In particular, direct emissions of chemicals related to chemical fertilizer showed a significant negative impact (Fig. 4). Especially in S4 and S5, more than 1.6 kg N eq per func- tional unit can be avoided when utilizing the nutrients from com- post/ solid digestate as a substitute for chemical fertilizer. The other high environmental credit was contributed by avoided elec- tricity in all scenarios with AD systems, which was mainly because using biogas produced electricity can replace electricity produced by fossil fuels. The EP of electricity produced by fossil fuels was 59.9 mg PO and 132.2 per KW (Pehnt, 2006; Evangelisti et al., 2014).
Except for S4, other systems had net negative ecotoxicity poten- tial (ETP). S3 had higher net ETP credits in comparison to the other

scenarios (Fig. 5). 56: The increasing demand of fossil fuel may lead in the future to a higher weightage of resource depletion damage. The resource depletion (RD) for the systems considered in this study ranges from -69.4 to 355.1 MJ surplus per functional unit (Fig. 6). Higher biomass transport required higher diesel consumption, while large

amount of corn stover used in S4 and S5 lead to higher environ- mental burdens in transportation process based on RD. Further- more, the electricity consumption in pretreatment process to chop corn stover and tomato residues would be more in S4 and S5 than other systems. Thus, S4 and S5 were not favorable option in terms of the net RD (355.1 and 353.1 MJ surplus per functional unit for S4 and S5). In comparison, S1 and S2 were good options in terms of the net RD (-69.4 and -70.3 MJ surplus per functional unit for S1 and S2).

Transportation processes were the main contributors to envi- ronmental burdens in terms of the RD. The transportation process contributed to 47%, 56%, 84%, 67%, and 64% of the RD environmen- tal burdens in S1-S5, respectively. Composting and AD process also have environmental burdens in terms of RD, which is mainly because of the material and energy consumptions related to the building of covered digestate tank. Avoided chemical fertilizer and avoided landfill (tomato residues) were the main processes having the environmental credits in terms of RD. Avoided chemical fertilizer contributed to 68% to 87% of environmental credit based on RD in all five systems. This was mainly because compost or digestate when used as organic fertilizer can avoid chemical fertil- izer such as phosphate. Since phosphate rock reserves are scarce (about 85 years availability of proved reserves at current extrac- tion rates; USGS, 2002) and phosphates are essential nutrients in crop production, a sustainable use of P resources is important. Fur- thermore, the present case study reveals clearly that the impact of arable farming on decreasing availability of exploitable phosphate rock resources is by far greater than that on decreasing availability

60: The life cycle impact assessment varies considerably with trans- portation distance for feedstock and compost/digestate (Table 5). The results from this study show that the differences in total ETP, and RD logistics is influenced by composting/AD plant loca- tion. For instance, for the scenario 2 where the composting/AD plant is located on the farmland, and also the farmland waste (e.g. corn stover) is the main feedstock in composting/AD. Thus, this scenario required less energy input for transportation, and shows advantage in all the life cycle impact categories. The envi- ronmental burdens of scenario 2 in terms of ETP is almost half than that of scenario 1 and 23% lower than scenario 3. Furthermore, the environmental burdens of RD in scenario 1 and scenario 3 are 42% and 11% fold higher than that in scenario 2, which indicated the higher benefit can be achieved by an appropriate composting / AD plant location. Transportation is not the key factor affecting AP, EP, and GWP (Figs. 2, 3 and 4), thus no significant difference exists for the 3 scenarios in terms of AP, EP, and GWP.
61: Potential integration of unutilized corn stover and tomato resi- due with dairy manure for anaerobic digestion and composting could reduce resource use and environmental impacts. Integrated solid state anaerobic digestion and composting showed the highest environmental credits on global warming potential while solid state anaerobic digestion of dairy manure, corn stover and tomato residues is the most favorable option in terms of acidification

potential, eutrophication potential, and ecotoxicity potential. There is a significant reduction in eutrophication potential, global warming potential, acidification potential, and ecotoxicity poten- tial, if anaerobic digestion is used prior to composting. The scenario analysis for transportation distance showed that the choice of loca- tion had a significant influence on the LCA outcomes. Locating the anaerobic digestion plant and composting facility on the farm was advantageous for all life cycle impact categories. Future studies need to focus on investigating the optimal conditions for on-farm organic waste treatment strategies with organic fertilizers (diges- tate and composting) application strategies for summer maize growing system.
